cv_text:
## PARIA	ALIPOUR

<!-- image -->

- <PHONE>

<EMAIL>

<!-- image -->

## PROFESSIONAL	SUMMARY

Bioinformatics	Scientist	with	a	PhD	in	Human	Genetics	and	extensive	experience	in	genomic	data	analysis,	highperformance	computing,	and	pipeline	development.	Skilled	in	analyzing	single-cell	and	bulk	RNA-seq,	WGS,	and proteomics	 data	 for	 translational	 and	 clinical	 research.	 Adept	 at	 interdisciplinary	 collaboration,	 scientific communication,	and	implementing	innovative	solutions	that	enhance	diagnostic	and	research	capacities.

## RESEARCH	EXPERIENCE

## Postdoctoral	Researcher

McGill	University,	<LOCATION>,	QC

January-	May	2025

- Quantified STMN2 splicing	 variants	in	single-cell	RNA-seq	data	from	Amyotrophic	Lateral	Sclerosis	(ALS) which	highlighted	significant	TDP-43	proteinopathy	in	ALS	spinal	cord	conducted	in	a	high-performance computing	(HPC)	environment.
- Estimated	ALS	risk	in	individuals	with	autoimmune	conditions	by	computing	Polygenic	Risk	Scores	(PRS)	and applying	unsupervised	machine	learning	approaches.
- Analyzed	whole	genome	sequencing	(WGS)	data	of	<LOCATION>-exposed	individuals	revealing	that	de	novo mutations	are	not	significantly	correlated	with	radiation	exposure.

## Bioinformatic	Scientist	Intern

35Pharma, <LOCATION>,	QC

October-	December	2024

- Performed	comprehensive	analysis	of	RNA-seq	datasets	across	preclinical	and	clinical	phases.
- Expanded	 analytical	 approaches	 to	 detect	 subtle	 changes	 in	 SomaScan	 proteomics	 data	 that	 cannot	 be detected	using	DEG	analysis,	enhancing	the	study	of	drug	effects.
- Evaluated	 the	 potentials	 to	 develop	 an	 in-house	 single-cell	 RNA-seq	 approach	 to	 enhance	 research capabilities.
- Established	 a	 database	 of	 biomarkers	 for	 complex	 conditions	 including	 heart	 failure,	 obesity,	 etc.,	 in collaboration	with	a	team	of	translational	scientists.

## Doctoral	Researcher

McGill	<LOCATION>, <LOCATION>,	<LOCATION>

September	2018	-	November	2024

- Conducted	analysis	of	large-scale	single-cell	RNA-seq	across	brain	and	spinal	cord	of	human	post-mortem tissues.
- Advanced	understanding	of	differential	vulnerability	in	ALS	by	Identifying	pathways	that	enhance	immune cell	survival	in	the	oculomotor	nucleus	compared	to	the	spinal	cord.
- Applied	mendelian	randomization	(MR)	to	refine	the	long-standing	hypothesis	of	a	relationship	between	ALS and	autoimmune	disorders.
- Mentored	two	graduate	students,	guiding	them	through	research	design,	data	analysis,	and	interpretation, fostering	their	academic	development.
- Collaborated	on	four	interdisciplinary	research	projects,	resulting	in	three	peer-reviewed	publications,	with one	manuscript	currently	in	preparation.

·

## Master	Level	Researcher

Shahrekord	University,	<LOCATION>,	<LOCATION>

September	2012	-	August	2015

- Conducted	annotation	of	Next-Generation	Sequencing	(NGS)	panel	data	for	Hearing	Loss	(HL)	samples.
- Identified	and	reported	two	novel	pathogenic	variants	in	HL	and	Ataxia-telangiectasia	disorders.

pitch_text:
## Document 2: Assignment 4 (Pitch)

## Selected Job Offer Position:

Bioinformatics Scientist, Oncology/Neuroscience

(Hypothetical Target: Roche or similar Biopharma) Key Requirements:

- PhD in Genetics or Bioinformatics.
- Experience with NGS, specifically Single-Cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq).
- Proficiency in R (<CANDIDATE_NAME>) and Python.
- Experience with biomarker discovery and multi-omics integration.

## The Elevator Pitch

"Hi, I'm <LOCATION>. I am a Bioinformatics Scientist who specializes in high-resolution genomic analysis for complex diseases.

I hold a PhD in Human Genetics from <CANDIDATE_NAME>, where I didn't just run standard pipelines-I built custom analytical approaches to detect subtle splicing variants in ALS single-cell data , handling the heavy computational lifting in an HPC environment.

I know that transiting from academia to industry requires more than just coding skills; it requires an understanding of drug development. That's why I recently completed an internship at 35Pharma , where I integrated RNA-seq with proteomics (SomaScan) to discover biomarkers for heart failure and obesity. I learned how to move fast, collaborate with translational scientists, and deliver data that drives decisions.

I've also taken recent interview feedback to heart and am currently advancing my skills in Machine Learning to better support predictive modeling teams. I'm looking for a role where I can combine my deep expertise in transcriptomics with my drive to solve clinical problems, and I think my background in both neurodegenerative and autoimmune disorders would be a great asset to your R&D team."

<LOCATION>

clarify_text.ikigai:
My Ikigai is decoding the complexity of human disease to drive precision medicine .

The central theme of my career is "Resolution." My passion lies in zooming in from the whole genome down to the single cell-using scRNA-seq and HPC pipelines to see what others miss (like the STMN2 splicing variants in ALS). My talent is the ability to handle massive, messy biological datasets and structure them into actionable insights.

The world needs reliable biomarkers and targeted therapies for complex conditions like ALS and autoimmune disorders. By intersecting my deep background in Human Genetics with my evolving skills in Data Science , I create a lasting impact: bridging the gap between raw sequencing data and a patient's diagnosis.

To align my career with this, I am actively pivoting from the "publication-focused" mindset of academia to the "product-focused" mindset of industry. I am currently upskilling in Machine Learning to ensure I can not only analyze past data but predict future biological responses, directly addressing the feedback I've received in recent interviews.
clarify_text.ideal_day:
My ideal day is a loop of computational rigor and biological discovery .

It starts with a "stand-up" meeting with an interdisciplinary team-biologists, clinicians, and data scientists. We discuss the results of an overnight pipeline run on the cluster. I thrive when I am the bridge, explaining why a specific <CANDIDATE_NAME> clustering parameter matters to the wet-lab scientists.

The core of the day is deep work: writing Python scripts or R code to integrate multi-omics data (proteomics + transcriptomics), similar to the work I did at 35Pharma. I enjoy the puzzle of optimizing a pipeline to run faster or cleaner.

In the late afternoon, I dedicate an hour to upskilling-perhaps applying a new Random Forest model to a dataset to refine my ML capabilities. The day ends with a sense of clarity, having turned a terabyte of raw reads into a clear biological hypothesis.
clarify_text.smart_goals:
- Goal 1 (Technical Gap Closure): Complete the "Advanced Machine Learning" course at NexaLand and build one portfolio project using a Deep Learning model on genomic data by April 2026.
- Measurable: GitHub repository created with code and documentation.
- Specific: Addresses the direct feedback from the Veracyte and Copoly interviews.
- Goal 2 (Career Placement): Secure a Bioinformatics Scientist role in a Biopharma company (Targeting companies like Roche, <LOCATION>, or 35Pharma) by June 2026.
- Measurable: Apply to 5 relevant positions per week; secure 2 second-round interviews per month.
- Attainable: Leveraging my recent internship experience to overcome the "entry-level" objection received at MUHC.
- Goal 3 (Publication/Productivity): Submit the manuscript currently in preparation regarding "Interdisciplinary research projects" to a peer-reviewed journal by May 2026.
- Measurable: Submission confirmation.
- Realistic: Addressing the "few first-author publications" feedback from <CANDIDATE_NAME> by finalizing pending work.

<LOCATION>

preferences.salary_expectations: Depending on the responsibilities and position I expect between 70k (for postdoc) and 120k (for others).
preferences.target_companies: I am interested in most of Biopharma company (any size) and also start-ups in the same field that works on treatment and diagnostics.
preferences.application_history: I mentioned in my interview experience.
preferences.recent_interviews:
I was invited for the below interviews. I have included the feedback:
1. Research Associate at McGill. 
Feedback: They mentioned that one limitation on my CV was the comparatively few first author publications. To some degree, this reflects productivity.
2. Bioinformatics Research Scientist @ Veracyte company
Feedback: They decided to pursue other candidate who has stronger computational experience. They feel I would be more suitable for R&D scientist positions. One of the most important skill they demanded was ML skill that I was not expert. I am taking a machine learning course now.
3. Two steps interviews for a Postdoc position at mount Saini. 
Feedback: I was accepted, but the visa and grant issues cancelled it. The prof also met my PhD supervisor, and he was super happy even after that.
4. Bioinformatician at MUHC, McGill.
Feedback: they invited me for the next step of the interview during the first interview, I did background check, but then, they did not move forward with me. During the interview session they mentioned different times that this is an entry-level position, and I might not be a good fit.
5. Bioinformatic scientist @ Billennium IT Inc (Roche)
Feedback: two rounds of interview, and no feedback.
6. Bioinformatician at 5Prime.
After two rounds of interview, I was rejected in technical interview. No feedback.
7. Computational Biologist @ Copoly
I did the first interview, but as he mentioned they needed someone expert in ophthalmology and ML for the other position. They did not invite me for the second round.
8. Postdoc at Postdoctoral researcher at University of Calgary
Feedback: I was not contacted after the first interview.
